Getting started with llegir

Source: vignettes/getting-started.Rmd
getting-started.Rmd

llegir is an R package to automate the interpretation of gene modules in a specific context. From an input gene expression dataset (single-cell, spatial transcriptomics, or bulk RNA-seq) and a set of gene modules, llegir gathers a standardized bundle of evidence per module using a pre-built toolbox of analysis functions, and drafts an evidence-backed interpretation via a LLM-enabled synthesis layer.

NOTE this package is at an experimental development stage, and much of the interface is subject to change.

Setup data and define the ModuleSet

In this vignette, we demonstrate llegir using a scRNA-seq dataset of myeloid cells from cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) cells profiled across several CNS conditions, with co-expression modules computed by hdWGCNA.

To start the llegir pipeline, we first define the ModuleSet, which contains the following attributes: modules(), gene_membership(), module_scores(), expression(), metadata(), capabilities(), pkg_versions(). The function hdWGCNA_ModuleSet() automatically formats the ModuleSet object for hdWGCNA results, but this framework is flexible to modules from other packages.

library(Seurat)
library(hdWGCNA)
library(tidyverse)
library(llegir)

# load the CSF dataset, which has already been processed with hdWGCNA
seurat_obj <- readRDS('data/CSF_Myeloid_hdWGCNA.rds')

# initialize the ModuleSet 
ms <- hdWGCNA_ModuleSet(seurat_obj)
modules(ms)
 [1] "MM1"  "MM2"  "MM3"  "MM4"  "MM5"  "MM6"  "MM7"  "MM8"  "MM9"  "MM10"
[11] "MM11" "MM12" "MM13" "MM14"

A quick look at the two attributes most tools lean on: the ranked gene membership of a module and the cell-level metadata.

head(gene_membership(ms, 'MM1'))
    gene_name module       kme
237     FCGBP    MM1 0.8274590
89        A2M    MM1 0.7623179
65      DOCK4    MM1 0.7402873
5        C1QB    MM1 0.7244926
4        C1QC    MM1 0.7211557
3        C1QA    MM1 0.7155457
head(metadata(ms))
                             nCount_RNA nFeature_RNA percent.mt project sample
CSF_01_4608_AAAGATGAGTGAAGAG       2229         1207   8.209960  CSF_01    S01
CSF_01_4608_AAAGCAAGTGCAGACA       1884         1037   6.475584  CSF_01    S01
CSF_01_4608_AAATGCCTCACGATGT       7446         2561   1.960784  CSF_01    S01
CSF_01_4608_AACACGTGTGCTTCTC       3612         1562   5.841639  CSF_01    S01
CSF_01_4608_AACCATGGTAAACGCG       8805         2361   2.759796  CSF_01    S01
CSF_01_4608_AACCATGGTACCGCTG       5416         1947   3.637371  CSF_01    S01
                             patient general_annot   lv1_annot
CSF_01_4608_AAAGATGAGTGAAGAG     P01       Myeloid          DC
CSF_01_4608_AAAGCAAGTGCAGACA     P01       Myeloid          DC
CSF_01_4608_AAATGCCTCACGATGT     P01       Myeloid          DC
CSF_01_4608_AACACGTGTGCTTCTC     P01       Myeloid Macrophages
CSF_01_4608_AACCATGGTAAACGCG     P01       Myeloid          DC
CSF_01_4608_AACCATGGTACCGCTG     P01       Myeloid          DC
                                            lv2_annot            diagnosis
CSF_01_4608_AAAGATGAGTGAAGAG DC non-inflammatory CD1C Primary CNS lymphoma
CSF_01_4608_AAAGCAAGTGCAGACA DC non-inflammatory CD1C Primary CNS lymphoma
CSF_01_4608_AAATGCCTCACGATGT DC non-inflammatory CD1C Primary CNS lymphoma
CSF_01_4608_AACACGTGTGCTTCTC                     BAMs Primary CNS lymphoma
CSF_01_4608_AACCATGGTAAACGCG               DC1 CLEC9A Primary CNS lymphoma
CSF_01_4608_AACCATGGTACCGCTG DC non-inflammatory CD1C Primary CNS lymphoma
                             CSF_collection_site metacell_grouping
CSF_01_4608_AAAGATGAGTGAAGAG              lumbar    S01#P01#CSF_01
CSF_01_4608_AAAGCAAGTGCAGACA              lumbar    S01#P01#CSF_01
CSF_01_4608_AAATGCCTCACGATGT              lumbar    S01#P01#CSF_01
CSF_01_4608_AACACGTGTGCTTCTC              lumbar    S01#P01#CSF_01
CSF_01_4608_AACCATGGTAAACGCG              lumbar    S01#P01#CSF_01
CSF_01_4608_AACCATGGTACCGCTG              lumbar    S01#P01#CSF_01

Orchestrating a chain of analysis tools

Each module is characterized by running a set of analysis tools. Every tool takes the same inputs (the ModuleSet, a module, and some parameters, bundled together as ctx) and returns one evidence_fragment: a standardized summary of what that tool found. To demonstrate this, we run a single tool on its own to see what it produces:

# set up the context (ctx) for the tool
ctx <- list(ms = ms, module_id = 'MM1', params = list(n_hubs = 10))

# run the hub genes tool to get the top 10 hub genes
hub_frag <- hub_genes_tool(ctx)

# sanity check:
validate_evidence_fragment(hub_frag)

# show the result
hub_frag$compact_summary
[1] "top 10 hub genes by kME: FCGBP, A2M, DOCK4, C1QB, C1QC, C1QA, TREM2, MSR1, EPB41L3, C5AR1"

llegir ships several pre-built analysis tools:

  • hub_genes_tool(): top hub genes by kME
  • module_by_metadata_tool(): association with a metadata column
  • cluster_dme_tool(): differential module expression across clusters
  • geneset_enrichment_tool(): GO/pathway enrichment

Additionally, external results you have already computed elsewhere can be leveraged the same way via import_fragment().

We run a chain of tools over the module set using run_orchestrator(), which then collects the results into one evidence packet per module, saved as a JSON file. The tool list pairs each tool (fn) with its parameters, and modules_use limits the run to three modules for this example:

modules_use <- c('MM1', 'MM2', 'MM3')

tool_config <- list(
    list(fn = hub_genes_tool, params = list(n_hubs = 25)),
    list(fn = cluster_dme_tool, params = list(group_by = 'lv2_annot')),
    list(fn = geneset_enrichment_tool, params = list(
        n_hubs = 25,
        db_files = c(
            GO_BP = 'data/GO_Biological_Process_2026.txt',
            Hallmark = 'data/h.all.v2026.1.Hs.symbols.gmt'
        )
    ))
)

packets <- run_orchestrator(ms, tool_config, output_dir = 'output/evidence_packets', modules_use = modules_use)
Warning in readLines(gmt.file): incomplete final line found on
'data/GO_Biological_Process_2026.txt'
Warning in readLines(gmt.file): incomplete final line found on
'data/GO_Biological_Process_2026.txt'
Warning in readLines(gmt.file): incomplete final line found on
'data/GO_Biological_Process_2026.txt'

Each packet holds its evidence fragments plus a packet_hash, a fingerprint of the evidence it contains. The same evidence always produces the same hash, which makes runs easy to track and reproduce.

packet <- packets[['MM1']]
packet$packet_hash
[1] "6f8c9d360b0491e09c421ca0c12d8ea1145a9449e82a07f51d1b43e1ce8aa906"
length(packet$fragments)
[1] 3

Dataset context

Before interpreting the modules, we provide a brief description of the dataset dataset_description(). This biological context is given to the model so it interprets each module in an appropriate setting (the same gene program can mean something different in CSF myeloid cells than in a solid tumor). It is required: llegir will not run synthesis without it.

desc <- dataset_description(
    species = 'human',
    tissue = 'cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)',
    cell_compartment = 'myeloid cells',
    assay = 'single-cell RNA-seq (10x)',
    conditions = c(
        'Glioblastoma', 'Brain Metastasis', 'Primary CNS lymphoma',
        'Secondary CNS lymphoma', 'Inflammatory / other neuroinflammatory'
    ),
    notes = 'Modules are CSF-myeloid co-expression programs; interpret in a CNS-myeloid, neuro-oncology / neuroinflammation context.'
)
cat(render_dataset_description(desc))
Dataset context:
- species: human
- tissue: cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)
- cell compartment: myeloid cells
- assay: single-cell RNA-seq (10x)
- conditions: Glioblastoma, Brain Metastasis, Primary CNS lymphoma, Secondary CNS lymphoma, Inflammatory / other neuroinflammatory
- notes: Modules are CSF-myeloid co-expression programs; interpret in a CNS-myeloid, neuro-oncology / neuroinflammation context.

Synthesis

run_synthesis_orchestrator() turns the evidence packets into written interpretations. For each module it sends the evidence to the model, checks that every claim is actually backed by the evidence (enforce_faithfulness()), and combines the model’s confidence with the evidence-based confidence (fuse_confidence()). It saves an interpretation and a readable Markdown paragraph per module, plus a review_queue.tsv and manifest.json for the whole run.

The model’s role is deliberately limited: it only ever sees the summarized evidence, never the raw data tables, and it never runs any analysis itself.

You can use different model providers through resolve_backend() (here 'github'; 'gemini' also works), which reads your API key from the environment (e.g. GITHUB_PAT or GEMINI_API_KEY). Wrapping it in cached_backend() saves each result to disk, so re-running synthesis on unchanged packets doesn’t spend another API call.

backend <- cached_backend(
    resolve_backend('github'),
    provider = 'github',
    model = 'gpt-4o-mini',
    prompt_template_version = PROMPT_TEMPLATE_VERSION
)

interps <- run_synthesis_orchestrator(packets, desc, backend = backend, output_dir = 'output/interpretations')

Inspect one interpretation: its proposed label, fused confidence, and the rendered paragraph.

interp <- interps[['MM1']]
interp$proposed_label
[1] "Myeloid Activation in CNS Tumors"
interp$confidence$score
[1] 0.9032809
cat(render_paragraph(interp))
**Myeloid Activation in CNS Tumors** (MM1)

Myeloid cell activation in response to CNS tumors and inflammation

Dominant biology: Myeloid cell activation in neuro-oncology
Primarily expressed in: Border TAMs
Condition dynamics: 

Supporting evidence:
- Myeloid cell states are significantly upregulated in the context of CNS tumors. (cluster_dme; direction: up)
- Genes associated with myeloid activation are enriched in biological processes related to immune response and synapse pruning. (geneset_enrichment; direction: up)

Metadata associations:
- cell_state: Strong expression in Border TAMs indicates a significant role in the module. (cluster_dme)

Confidence: 0.90 -- The evidence indicates a strong upregulation of myeloid cell states and significant enrichment in relevant biological processes, supporting the proposed label. [fusion: model=0.85, evidence=0.96 (effect=0.87, enrichment=1.00, agreement=1.00/convergent_signal), fused=0.90]

The review queue (build_review_queue()) collects only the interpretations flagged for a closer look (needs_review()), sorted lowest-confidence first, so you know which modules to check by hand:

build_review_queue(interps)
    module_id confidence                 flags
MM2       MM2      0.826 insufficient_evidence
                                                                               reason
MM2 deterministic evidence signals are weak (low effect size / few significant terms)
